data.title: If You Invested $1000 In Costco Wholesale Stock 5 Years Ago, You Would Have This Much Today
data.description:
Costco Wholesale (NASDAQ:COST) has outperformed the market over the past 5 years by 14.15% on an annualized basis producing an average annual return of 25.12%. Currently, Costco Wholesale has a market capitalization of $443.96 billion.

Buying $1000 In COST: If an investor had bought $1000 of COST stock 5 years ago, it would be worth $3,035.67 today based on a price of $1000.20 for COST at the time of writing.

Costco Wholesale's Performance Over Last 5 Years

Finally -- what's the point of all this? The key insight to take from this article is to note how much of a difference compounded returns can make in your cash growth over a period of time.

data.title: How One Trader Flipped $50K Into $520K — and What You Can Learn About Managing Trades
data.description:
A trader on Reddit's r/wallstreetbets said they grew a brokerage account from $50,000 to more than $520,000 in less than a year, using margin to buy shares rather than relying on the short-dated options trades more commonly associated with the forum.

The post offered a detailed breakdown of the trades that drove the gains and the strategy behind them.

"My first big trade was buying $SBET at $9 in early 2025, which turned my account into around $200k," the trader wrote.

That position in Sharplink Inc (NASDAQ:SBET) appears to have provided the capital for a series of later trades across multiple names and sectors.

The post stood out in part because of the trader's stated preference for margin over options.

"I prefer margin over options," they wrote. "No time decay. If something chops sideways or down you don't automatically lose like with options"

That reasoning reflects a basic distinction between the two approaches. Options lose value as expiration approaches, even if the underlying stock does not move much. Buying shares on margin still introduces leverage and risk, but it removes the fixed expiration that can quickly turn an otherwise correct idea into a loss.

That point drew pushback from other users.

"You say no options as if margin isn't much stupider," one commenter wrote in a reply that received nearly 800 upvotes.

The post also outlined a method for taking profits. Rather than treating gains as an all-or-nothing outcome, the trader described using preset targets to trim positions over time.

"Use AI to help set price targets and profit goals," they advised. "It helps remove emotion and gives you levels where you can start trimming instead of panic selling".

The trader also said they generated additional income by selling covered calls against stock positions that had already appreciated.

"Covered calls are free income," they wrote. "If you're already up big on shares you can sell calls to people gambling on weeklies"

The account's growth from $50,000 to more than $520,000 is unusual, and the use of margin adds real downside if positions move the wrong way. But the post offers a useful look at how some retail traders think about risk when they are not relying entirely on speculative options trades.

The gains came from a mix of concentrated positions, leverage, timing and profit-taking. The larger point is not that margin is safe or that such returns are typical. It is that the trader appeared to follow a framework, with defined entries, a willingness to exit and a clear preference for instruments that gave positions more time to play out. 

For traders who already have a playbook but would rather test it on a simulated account with firm-style drawdown limits—and get paid a share of profits if their strategy actually performs—models like Apex's evaluation and Performance Accounts can be one way to see whether that edge holds up without continually refilling a personal brokerage account.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: Gold Is Down 3% Since The Iran War Started—Time To Buy The Dip?
data.description:
Gold has fallen roughly 3% since the U.S. and Israel struck Iran on Feb. 28, even as Brent crude has surged nearly 40% toward $100 a barrel.

The SPDR Gold Shares (NYSE:GLD) is sitting around $468 while the S&P 500 slides to its lowest since November.

Why Gold Falls First In A War

Giovanni Staunovo, a strategist at UBS Global Wealth Management who was bullish on gold throughout its 64% surge in 2025, told CNBC the drop is textbook.

“Historically, it is not uncommon to see gold falling as first reaction when financial markets show stress signs as gold is a highly liquid asset,” Staunovo said.

When panic hits, investors sell what they can liquidate fast. The dollar has surged to a three-month high and Treasury yields have climbed, raising the cost of holding a zero-yield asset.

“Market participants are translating higher oil prices in rising inflation and central banks turning more hawkish,” he added.

Then It Soars

Staunovo expects it to be temporary. He said this week that gold will soon regain its role as a safe-haven asset and projected $6,200 per ounce by June, roughly 20% above current levels. UBS entered 2025 forecasting $2,900 gold and kept raising targets as the metal ran.

Polymarket traders think there is a 29% chance of $6,200 gold by the end of June, and a 16% chance of $6,500.

On Polymarket’s ceasefire market, traders give 54% odds of a U.S.-Iran ceasefire by May 31.

On Kalshi, recession odds have surged from 22% to roughly 31%.

Goldman Sachs raised its year-end PCE inflation forecast by 0.8 percentage points to 2.9% this week. Stagflation is historically the best backdrop for gold.

Image: Shutterstock
